main_sections.title: Feathered Friend
main_sections.prose_summary:
Feathered Friend is a science fiction short story by Arthur C. Clarke, narrated in the first person by an unnamed officer aboard a space station. The story opens by establishing that no regulation forbade pets on the station, though no one had thought to make one either. Sven Olsen, one of the station's best construction workers — skilled at manoeuvring steel girders in free fall — had smuggled aboard a small yellow canary named Claribel, hiding her during the ferry journey from Earth. His stated reason was scientific curiosity: he wanted to observe how a bird would behave in zero gravity. Claribel quickly adapted, learning to float and loop through the station with effortless grace. She became a communal favourite, concealed from visiting VIPs in the station's many hiding places, though her occasional noisy peeps from ventilating shafts caused some anxious moments.

The story shifts to a morning when Claribel is found unconscious — two clenched claws pointing pathetically upwards. The ship's cook-doctor-dietitian, Jock Duncan, cannot confirm a heartbeat. The crew revives her with oxygen from an emergency cylinder, but she collapses again immediately after perching on Sven's finger and giving her signature trill. The narrator, whose mind feels sluggish and headachy, suddenly recalls that miners historically carried canaries into coal mines to detect dangerous gases. He alerts the duty engineer Jim, who dismisses the warning confidently — until his assistant points out that the second alarm circuit was never connected. Jim leaves without a word. The air purifier had frozen during a rare eclipse of Earth's shadow, and the single connected alarm had silently failed. Half a million dollars of engineering had been defeated; without Claribel, the crew would have died. The narrator ends with a wry recommendation: any space station visitor who hears an inexplicable snatch of birdsong should feel doubly reassured rather than alarmed — it means the station has a living early-warning system at practically no extra cost. The story's central theme is the value of unexpected, low-tech solutions in high-tech environments, and the quiet heroism of a creature whose sensitivity to danger saved an entire crew.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. Work in pairs and discuss the questions given below. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: Who can a 'feathered' friend be?
Which 'feathered' friend do you like? Why?
If you have a choice between a 'feathered' and a 'furry' friend, who would you choose? Why?
Do you think a 'feathered' or a 'furry' friend can accompany astronauts to space? If yes, why? If no, why not?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Complete the words given below by inserting vowels. Refer to their meanings given alongside. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: official rule that controls how something is done: R _ G _ L _ T _ _ N
to not allow something: F _ R B _ D
join or blend to form a single entity: F _ S _
suddenly: _ B R _ P T L Y
admit one's mistake: C _ N F _ S S
a person who advises what to eat to keep healthy: D_ _ T _ T _ _ N

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions briefly.

question_bank.stem: Why did the narrator think Sven had sneaked a bird aboard? What was Sven's scientific reason?
How did Claribel adjust to the new surroundings?
What made the narrator mistake the musical whistle for a sound from the intercom?
Do you think the presence of the canary would lead the spacers into trouble? If yes, why? If no, why not?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Identify whether the following statements from parts I and II are true or false. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem: The alarm at the space station failed to work properly because they were not connected.
Claribel was brought to the space station for an official experiment on animal behaviour in space.
The canary's suffering helped the crew discover a problem with the air purifier.
The air purifier froze because there was a rare eclipse.
Claribel could fly gracefully in the space station, performing loops in the air.
The space station crew was uninterested in Claribel and hardly noticed her presence after she was discovered.
Claribel had fainted and had to be revived with oxygen supply.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: Sven was one of our best construction men, and excelled at the tricky and specialised work of collecting assorted girders as they floated around in free fall, making them do the slow-motion, three-dimensional ballet that would get them into their right positions, and fusing the pieces together when they were precisely dovetailed into the intended pattern: it was a skilled and difficult job, for a space suit is not the most convenient of garbs in which to work.

question_bank.stem:
What can be inferred about Sven's abilities from his job description?
A. He is good at following instructions but lacks creativity.
B. He possesses a strong understanding of working in space.
C. He struggles with the complexities of construction in space.
D. He prefers working on simpler tasks rather than complex jobs.
How can we conclude that Sven was working in zero gravity?

Select the option that is correct for both Assertion (A) and Reason (R).
(A): Sven's job required him to make girders perform a 'three-dimensional ballet' in space.
(R): Working in zero gravity allows objects to float, making precise positioning more challenging.
A. Both (A) and (R) are true, and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).
B. Both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).
C. (A) is true, but (R) is false.
D. (A) is false, but (R) is true.
Replace the underlined word with a word similar in meaning from the extract. During the festival, people wore colourful outfits that represented their culture. [underlined: outfits]

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 2: "Jim! There's something wrong with the air! That's why Claribel's passed out. I've just remembered that miners used to carry canaries down to warn them of gas." "Nonsense!" said Jim. "The alarms would have gone off. We've got duplicate circuits, operating independently." "Er—the second alarm circuit isn't connected up yet," his assistant reminded him. That shook Jim; he left without a word, while we stood arguing and passing the oxygen bottle around like a pipe of peace.

question_bank.stem: Fill in the blank by selecting the correct option from those given in the brackets. The narrator references canaries and miners in order to __________. (highlight the historical use of canaries as warning systems / explain the reason that caused Claribel to faint suddenly)
Complete the following sentence with a suitable reason. Jim initially dismisses the warning about the air because __________.

What does the phrase 'shook Jim' mean in the context of the information in the extract? Jim was _________.
A. physically pushed by someone
B. startled by unexpected information
C. cold and needed to warm up
D. trying to shake off sleepiness
What does the crew's action of passing the oxygen bottle around 'like a pipe of peace' suggest about their response to the situation?

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: What was the purpose of the alarm? What had caused its failure?
How does the crew's reaction to Claribel change over the course of the story?
What does the last part of the story tell us about the significance of unusual ways of problem solving?
Justify the appropriateness of the title of the story.
'Without Claribel, we should soon have been slightly dead.' What does this line suggest about uncertainty of life in space?
Explain how has the writer presented science fiction with a touch of humour.
How would Sven reflect on his experience of almost losing Claribel and for saving the lives of the entire crew?

listening.transcript_text:
Monika: Hi, Toby! Do you miss your planet?
Toby: Very much, Monika! I miss my people and home.
Monika: Oh! I wish I could do something about it! But I am happy to have you as my friend.
Toby: Thank you, Monika!
Monika: Er... could you tell me something more about yourself?
Toby: Why not! What do you want to know about me, Monika?
Monika: What do you eat for breakfast?
Toby: Well, we Martians don't need to eat anything. We get our energy from the Sun.
Monika: Oh! Don't you ever feel like having ice creams and chocolates? I can't think of life without them!
Toby: No, Monika! We are made of different stuff.
Monika: Okay, tell me one thing. How are you able to see with only one eye? I see only one big eye in the centre of your face.
Toby: My friend, we Martians need only one eye to see clearly. In fact, I can see even with my eye closed. I can see beyond mountains and clouds, wood and even metals. I can see that you are carrying a toy in your pocket. It's a blue aeroplane. Isn't it? And yes! That apple you just had for breakfast is going round-and-round in your stomach right now!
Monika: (gasps in disbelief) Oh my goodness!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a conversation between Monika and Toby. Toby is a Martian and is stranded on Earth. He has become Monika's friend. As you listen, put a tick mark against the correct statements and a cross against the wrong ones.

question_bank.stem: Toby misses his school.
Monika accepts Toby as her friend.
Monika asks Toby about one of his facial features.
Toby tells Monika that she had a banana for breakfast.
Toby shares that he can see much clearer with a closed eye.

Toby: Well, we _________________ don't need to eat anything. We get our _________________ from the Sun.
Monika: Oh! Don't you ever feel like having ice-creams and _________________!
Monika: Okay, tell me one thing. How are you able to see only with _________________ eye?
Toby: I can see beyond mountains and _________________, woods and even ____________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: 'A canary in the coal mine' is a popular phrase to describe early signs of potential danger. Read about how coal miners in places like Arunachal Pradesh used canary birds to detect the presence of carbon monoxide. These birds are particularly sensitive to carbon monoxide and can prevent mining accidents.

Astronauts living in space stations have to do things differently. Read how they manage the routine tasks in the absence of gravity (Write, Exercise, Eat, Sleep). You may find out more information about it from the internet.

Did you know that animals can save humans from natural calamities? Animals can sense natural calamities. Read the following examples and decide if this is true: elephants, flamingos, dogs before 2004 tsunami; oxen, sheep, dogs, geese before Naples quake 1805; migratory birds dodging storms; sharks and dolphins sensing hurricanes.
Read about Salim Ali, the 'Birdman of India'—India's first ornithologist to conduct systematic bird surveys. He was awarded the Padma Bhushan (1958) and the Padma Vibhushan (1976).
We need to conserve our 'feathered' friends. Work in groups of four. Look at the pictures and make a bird feeder with cardboard, chart paper, etc.

main_sections.title: Magnifying Glass
main_sections.poem_text:
With this round glass
I can make Magic talk—
A myriad shells show
In a scrap of chalk;
Of but an inch of moss
A forest—flowers and trees;
A drop of water
Like hive of bees.

I lie in wait and watch
How the deft spider jets
The woven web-silk
From his spinnerets;
The tigerish claws he has!
And oh! the silly flies
The stumble into his net—
With all those eyes!

Not even the tiniest thing
But this my glass
Will make more marvellous
And itself surpass.

Yes, and with lenses like it,
Eyeing the moon,
'Twould seem you'd walk there
In an afternoon!

Walter de la Mare
main_sections.poem_appreciation_summary: Magnifying Glass by Walter de la Mare celebrates the transformative power of close observation through a simple lens. The speaker delights in how the magnifying glass turns the ordinary into the extraordinary: a scrap of chalk reveals a myriad of shells, an inch of moss becomes a forest, a drop of water teems like a hive of bees, and a spider's web becomes an intricate, almost predatory marvel complete with tigerish claws and multifaceted eyes. The tone is one of wonder and playful curiosity, with exclamations and vivid imagery sustaining a child-like sense of amazement throughout. The poem employs simile ('Like hive of bees'), metaphor ('Magic talk'), alliteration ('woven web-silk'), and personification to animate the world seen through the glass. The final stanza extends the metaphor from tiny objects to the celestial — lenses like the magnifying glass could bring the moon within walking distance — suggesting that scientific tools infinitely expand human perception and possibility.

Which is your favourite part of the poem? Why?

listening.transcript_text:
Daughter: Papa, everyone talks about how yoga and the concept of zero came from India, but I recently found out there are so many more inventions that people don't really know about!
Father: Oh really? Like what?
Daughter: Well, did you know that radio broadcasting has roots in India? We usually credit Marconi, but Jagadish Chandra Bose, a Bengali scientist, actually conducted an experiment using microwaves before Marconi.
Father: Wow, I didn't know that! That's incredible. I can recollect something about fibre optics.
Daughter: Yes! You're absolutely right Papa. Can you imagine life without fibre optics? No fast internet, no clear communication… And guess what? It was pioneered by Narinder Kapany, an Indian physicist from Punjab. He's called the 'father of fibre optics.'
Father: That's something I use every day, but I just had an idea that the origins were in India. What about fun things? Any of those that you've found out about?
Daughter: Yes indeed! You'll love this one—Snakes and Ladders! It was actually invented as a game to teach children values, with ladders representing virtues and snakes representing evil. It wasn't just a board game like we think today. It had a spiritual meaning in ancient times.
Father: So even that simple game had such deep roots. Let me tell you about one that I just remembered.
Daughter: Of course! I'd love to know.
Father: Well, the USB port, something we rely on all the time to connect devices, was invented by Ajay Bhatt. He's an Indian-born engineer who helped revolutionise the way we use technology.
Daughter: That's amazing. USB is such a basic part of life now. It's hard to imagine a time without it.
Father: That's incredible. And all this from India. I definitely learned a lot from this conversation.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a conversation between a father and daughter about lesser-known Indian inventions. As you listen, mark the four true statements from (1)–(6) given below.

question_bank.stem: The father is surprised by the fact that India was involved in the invention of radio broadcasting and fibre optics.
The daughter appears indifferent when learning the origins of the USB port.
The father shows little interest in the information about the origin of snakes and ladders.
The father was simply confirming all the facts shared by the daughter.
The daughter expresses pride and admiration for India's role in global inventions.
The daughter is excited as she shares new discoveries about India's contributions with her father.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
When pronouncing /v/ and /f/, the lower lip lightly touches the edge of the upper teeth. Note the difference between /v/ and /f/. Read these words aloud with the help of your teacher.
beginning sound /f/: forest, flowers, from
middle sound /f/: deft, afternoon
end sound /f/: of, itself
beginning sound /v/: vase, valour, victory
middle sound /v/: woven, even
end sound /v/: hive, marvellous

If you could invent something, what would you like to invent and how? Give reasons for the choice of your invention. Speak about your wonder invention.

The poet uses the expression 'eyeing the moon' in the poem. Match the idiomatic expressions with 'eye' given in Column 1 with their meanings in Column 2. You may refer to a dictionary.
1. apple of one's eye — (v) a person who is very precious or important
2. in the blink of an eye — (iii) something that happens very quickly
3. keep an eye on something or somebody — (i) watch something or someone closely
4. turn a blind eye — (ii) act as if you do not see or notice
5. see eye to eye — (vi) agree with each other
6. bird's-eye view — (iv) an overall look at something

question_bank.stem: Choose the nouns from the list that can take the suffix '-ish' to make adjectives: girl, glass, book, silk, boy, water, moon, scrap.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Children with low vision may benefit from different types of visual aids, such as magnifying spectacles, stand magnifiers, hand-held magnifiers, and telescopes. Read about the different types and their uses.

Did you know that telescopes also use lenses? Read about: (1) the largest telescope in India in Nainital, Uttarakhand (ARIES, 2016); (2) the Indian Astronomical Observatory (IAO) in Hanle, Ladakh at 4,500m elevation; (3) the James Webb Space Telescope (NASA, Dec 2021, 1.5 million km from Earth); (4) Mauna Kea Observatory in Hawaii. Find out more about them from the internet and discuss with your science teacher and classmates.

Read about ancient Indian scholars: (1) Āryabhaṭa — Āryabhaṭiyam, heliocentric model, lunar and solar eclipses; (2) Varāmihira — Pañchasiddhāntika, sixth century CE; (3) Bhāskarachārya II — Siddhāntaśiromaṇi and Karaṇakutūhala, tenth century CE.

Read about Jantar Mantar in New Delhi — an astronomical observatory built in 1724 by Maharaja Sawai Jai Singh II, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. Five observatories were built in Ujjain, Mathura, Varanasi, Jaipur, and New Delhi.

main_sections.title: Bibha Chowdhuri — The Beam of Light that Lit the Path for Women in Indian Science
main_sections.prose_summary: This informational piece profiles Bibha Chowdhuri (1913–1991), India's first woman physicist, celebrating her contributions to high-energy particle physics and her trailblazing role for women in STEM. Born in Kolkata in pre-independent India, Bibha defied societal expectations and gender barriers to earn a place at the Bose Institute and become the first Indian woman to excel in high-energy particle physics. In 1945 she moved to the University of Manchester to study under Nobel Laureate Patrick M.S. Blackett, focusing on cosmic rays. Her Ph.D. thesis earned her recognition in the press, which dubbed her 'India's New Woman Scientist — She has an eye for Cosmic Rays.' Her most notable contribution was the discovery of pi-mesons (a subatomic particle), and she was nominated for a Nobel Prize in 1950 by Erwin Schrödinger — though the nomination was unsuccessful. Upon returning to India in 1949, she became the first woman faculty member at the Tata Institute of Fundamental Research (TIFR), personally selected by Homi J. Bhabha. She subsequently worked at the Physical Research Laboratory in Ahmedabad under Vikram Sarabhai's mentorship and at the Saha Institute of Nuclear Physics in Kolkata. Despite decades of crucial research, she received no awards in her lifetime and passed away in 1991 largely forgotten. Her legacy was formally recognised in 2019 when the International Astronomical Union renamed a star in the constellation Leo — HD 86081 — as 'Bibha,' meaning 'beam of light.' In 2020 the Government of India declared a chair professorship in her name. The text then connects her pioneering spirit to modern women scientists at ISRO, highlighting Dr. Ritu Karidhal Srivastava ('Rocket Woman of India') and the over 50 women who contributed to Chandrayaan-3, arguing that Bibha Chowdhuri's quiet determination laid the foundation for India's women in science today.

reading_for_comprehension.section_name: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Let us do these activities before we read. Work in pairs. What qualities do you associate with a scientist? Share your answers with your classmates and teacher. Complete the word web given below.

question_bank.stem: What qualities do you associate with a scientist? Complete the word web.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Some pictures of women scientists are given below. What do you know about their work? Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.
Arrange the following events from Bibha Chowdhuri's life in the correct order of occurrence. Share your answers with your classmates and teacher.

question_bank.stem:
Arrange in correct chronological order:
1. The IAU honoured her legacy by renaming a star as 'Bibha'.
2. She became the first woman faculty member at TIFR.
3. Bibha Chowdhuri was born in pre-independent India.
4. The Government of India declared a chair professorship in Bibha's name.
5. She joined the University of Manchester under Patrick M.S. Blackett.
6. She was nominated for a Nobel Prize by Erwin Schrödinger.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 1: In 1945, Bibha's academic journey took her to the University of Manchester. Under the guidance of the celebrated Nobel Laureate, Patrick M.S. Blackett, she delved deeper into the field of cosmic rays. Her Ph.D. thesis on cosmic rays earned local recognition, with newspapers introducing her as 'India's New Woman Scientist—She has an eye for Cosmic Rays'.

question_bank.stem: Why is the mention of Patrick M.S. Blackett, a Nobel Laureate, significant in this extract?

What can be inferred about the public perception of women scientists in the 1940s from the title given to her by the press 'India's New Woman Scientist'?
A. Women scientists were widely accepted and celebrated at that time.
B. The world was still doubtful about the capabilities of women scientists.
C. Women scientists were as common and renowned as men in the 1940s.
D. Women were not allowed to study sciences or pursue scientific careers.

In the phrase 'celebrated Nobel Laureate,' the word 'celebrated' refers to all of the following EXCEPT someone who is:
A. famous and highly respected
B. frequently seen in public
C. renowned for their achievements
D. well-regarded in their field
Complete the following sentence suitably. The phrase 'She has an eye for Cosmic Rays' suggests that __________.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the given extracts and answer the questions that follow.

Extract 2: The journey from Bibha Chowdhuri's era to today's women scientists has been long and arduous, but it is also inspiring. The struggles Chowdhuri faced are a stark reminder of how far we've come—and how much further we have to go… Bibha Chowdhuri's legacy lives on. Her story continues to inspire young women to pursue their passions, break barriers, and leave their mark on the world.

question_bank.stem:
What can be inferred about the challenges faced by women scientists today, as compared to Bibha Chowdhuri's era?
A. Women scientists no longer face any barriers.
B. The challenges remain, but progress has been made.
C. The struggles have become more difficult over time.
D. The struggles are the same as they were for Chowdhuri.
The phrase 'Bibha Chowdhuri's legacy lives on' suggests that __________.
Why is Bibha Chowdhuri referred to as a 'beacon' and a 'beam of light' in the extract?
State whether the following sentence is a fact or an opinion: 'Bibha Chowdhuri was the beacon, the beam of light whose story continues to inspire young women.'

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Answer the following questions.

question_bank.stem: Why is Bibha Chowdhuri considered a pioneer in the field of science?
What does Bibha Chowdhuri's nomination by Erwin Schrödinger tell us about her abilities?
Bibha Chowdhuri never worked for awards or recognition. Support this statement with evidence from the text.
How does the renaming of a star as 'Bibha' serve as both a literal and symbolic recognition of her contributions to science?
How do the roles of women in ISRO today reflect broader changes in societal attitudes towards women in STEM in India?
What is the writer's purpose of highlighting the contributions of Bibha Chowdhuri and other modern women scientists?
How might this text help shape people's perception of the role of women in other traditionally male-dominated fields?

listening.transcript_text:
Hello and welcome to Tech Talks, the podcast where we explore how technology is shaping our world! I'm Faizy, your host, and today we're diving into the fascinating topic of artificial intelligence, or AI, and how it's benefiting humankind.
AI is often seen as futuristic, but it's already making everyday tasks easier and more efficient. Let's look at some of the incredible ways AI is improving our lives in four of the many segments.
Segment 1: Healthcare Transformation. AI systems can analyse medical data much faster and more accurately than humans, leading to quicker diagnoses and personalised treatments. AI-powered robots are also assisting in surgeries, making them more precise and less invasive. And during the global pandemic, AI played a crucial role in speeding up the discovery of new medicines.
Segment 2: Environmental Sustainability. AI is helping us fight climate change. It helps in predicting natural disasters, and managing resources like water and agriculture more efficiently. In renewable energy, AI is improving the performance of wind turbines and solar panels, pushing us toward a greener future.
Segment 3: Enhancing Education. In education, AI is offering personalised learning experiences to students. Teachers also benefit from AI, which helps analyse student performance and manage administrative tasks, giving them more time to focus on teaching.
Segment 4: Accessibility and Inclusion. AI is also improving accessibility for people with disabilities. Technologies like speech recognition, text-to-speech, and voice commands make communication easier and faster.
Now you know how AI is improving life for humankind. AI is a powerful force for good but we need to use it responsibly. Thanks for tuning in to Tech Talks. Join us next time for another deep dive into the world of technology. Until then, stay curious, and keep exploring!

tasks_verbatim.task_text: You will listen to a podcast about Artificial Intelligence. As you listen, answer the following questions by selecting the correct options.

question_bank.stem:
What is one of the key benefits of AI in the healthcare sector?
(i) Helps doctors communicate faster with patients.
(ii) Provides personalised treatments and faster diagnoses.
(iii) Focuses on cosmetic surgeries to improve appearance.

How did AI play a crucial role during the global pandemic?
(i) AI robots monitored patient behaviour in hospitals.
(ii) AI assisted in speeding up the discovery of new medicines.
(iii) AI developed different types of vaccines by itself.

What example was given regarding AI's role in renewable energy?
(i) AI is optimising the placement of wind turbines and solar panels in cities.
(ii) AI is promoting alternative energy from wind turbines and solar panels.
(iii) AI is improving the performance of wind turbines and solar panels.

What does AI offer to teachers in the field of education?
(i) Helps teachers assign grades more quickly.
(ii) Allows teachers to communicate better with students.
(iii) Helps teachers analyse student performance.

What is one of the user-friendly features that AI is enhancing for people with disabilities?
(i) Helps them find better jobs in the mainstream.
(ii) Improves speech recognition and voice command technologies.
(iii) Trains individuals to interact with robots.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Form a group of five. Conduct a panel discussion on the topic, 'Evaluating the Impact of Mobile Phones—Beneficial or Harmful'. Each student will take on one of the following roles: Moderator, Principal (opposed), Teacher (partially in favour), Parent (opposed), Student (in favour). Use the cues provided for each role.

Your school recently hosted the Zonal Science Exhibition, in which teams of students from 25 schools participated enthusiastically. As the student editor, write a report on the exhibition to be published in your school magazine. Use the cues given below with your own ideas to compose this report.
Points to remember: (1) Write the report in past tense, passive voice, and third person. (2) Follow proper format with a headline, reporter's name, and three paragraphs.
Cues: organiser/host; date/time/venue; purpose; participants; Chief Guest; competitions (Science Quiz, Science Models etc.); topics of models; winning team; observations of Chief Guest and visitors.

Complete the crossword puzzle by filling in the antonyms from the clues given below.
Across: 4. core → ?; 5. dependent → ?; 7. extinguish → ?; 8. terrestrial → ?
Down: 1. effortless → ?; 2. weakness → ?; 3. neglect → ?; 6. trivial → ?

question_bank.stem: Complete the crossword with antonyms. Across: 4. core, 5. dependent, 7. extinguish, 8. terrestrial. Down: 1. effortless, 2. weakness, 3. neglect, 6. trivial.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:

2. A scientist who studies matter, energy, light, etc.

5. My brother finally succeeded in his business because of his continuous efforts and determination.

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read about Clipping — a method of making words from larger words in English. Three types: Back clipping (exam—examination), Front clipping (phone—telephone), Middle clipping (flu—influenza). The short form of laboratory is lab (back clipping).

Read the sentence from the text: '…the foundation was laid by early pioneers like Bibha Chowdhuri…' Study the table about active and passive voice. Now, complete the following news report with the passive form of verbs given in the box below: discover, test, measure, publish, record, take.

question_bank.stem:
Complete the news report using passive forms of: discover, test, measure, publish, record, take.
Last year, significant advancements in medical science 1. __________ in leading medical journals when a new vaccine for malaria 2. __________. The vaccine 3. __________ through several clinical trials and results 4. _________ in those journals. During the trials, blood samples 5. __________ regularly from participants to monitor their immune response. The amount of antibodies produced 6. __________ carefully to assess the effectiveness of the vaccine.

tasks_verbatim.task_text:
Read the following report of an experiment. Rewrite the report using passive verbs wherever necessary. You may begin like this: An experiment was conducted…
(Original: I conducted an experiment to investigate the effects of light on plant growth. I placed various plants under different light conditions and measured their growth over several weeks. I recorded data daily to track the height of each plant. At the end of the experiment, I analysed the results and found that plants exposed to natural light grew significantly taller than those under artificial light. I concluded that sunlight plays a crucial role in plant development. I presented my findings to the class, highlighting the importance of light in enhancing photosynthesis and overall plant health.)
Read the instructions given in the box on landing of Chandrayaan. Complete the passage by choosing the correct answer from the options given.

question_bank.stem:
Chandrayaan's landing mission involves several key steps. First, the spacecraft 1.________ towards the Moon from the Earth.
(i) A. launched  B. were launched  C. was launched  D. is launched

Then, it 2. ________ to enter the Moon's orbit.
(ii) A. position  B. was positioned  C. is positioned  D. has positioned

After reaching the Moon's orbit, the spacecraft's path 3. ________ for landing.
(iii) A. adjusted  B. was adjusted  C. is adjusted  D. were adjusted

Next, the spacecraft 4. ________ as it nears the Moon's surface.
(iv) A. slowed down  B. was slowed down  C. is slowed down  D. were slowed down

Finally, the rover 5.________ to explore the surface and collect valuable data.
(v) A. is released  B. has released  C. were released  D. was released

tasks_verbatim.task_text: Read about Augmented Reality (AR) and Virtual Reality (VR) and how they are changing the way students learn. NCERT has developed the e-Pathshala AR App under MHRD, Government of India. This App enables students to go beyond textbooks and learn concepts by directly experimenting. Visit: https://ciet.ncert.gov.in/ar-vr

Read about the Government of India's new set of National Awards in Science, Technology, and Innovation — 'Rashtriya Vigyan Puraskar' (RVP): Vigyan Ratna (VR), Vigyan Shri (VS), Vigyan Yuva-Shanti Swarup Bhatnagar (VY-SSB), and Vigyan Team (VT) awards.
A glimpse of women pioneers from different fields. Read about and discuss women scientists from different domains in India.
